title: How do you see the world?
description: Rate how much you agree or disagree with each statement. There are no right or wrong answers. Answer instinctively — your first reaction is usually the most honest one.

archetypes.name: Adam Smith
archetypes.tagline: Free markets, free minds, guided by evidence.
archetypes.description: You believe in individual enterprise and limited government, and you trust data over rhetoric. Adam Smith argued that markets self-regulate through self-interest — but also wrote The Theory of Moral Sentiments, showing that social conscience matters too. Your strength is clear-eyed analysis. Your risk: underestimating what markets leave behind.

archetypes.name: Thomas Paine
archetypes.tagline: Fiercely free, impossibly honest, and louder than the room.
archetypes.description: You champion personal liberty with passion and are suspicious of authority in all forms. Thomas Paine wrote Common Sense in six weeks and changed a continent. You move people with conviction rather than charts. Your strength is moral clarity under pressure. Your risk: passion can outrun precision.

archetypes.name: Otto von Bismarck
archetypes.tagline: Order, results, and the pragmatism to get both.
archetypes.description: You believe strong central authority and market freedom are not contradictions — they are complements. Bismarck unified Germany, invented the welfare state, and ran the most powerful nation in Europe with ruthless efficiency. You value results over ideology. Your strength is building things that last. Your risk: efficiency without justice.

archetypes.name: Edmund Burke
archetypes.tagline: Society is a contract between the dead, the living, and the unborn.
archetypes.description: You believe in preserving what works, respecting authority that has earned its place, and are deeply moved by the moral stakes of political decisions. Burke opposed both revolution and tyranny — and wept when he saw both ignored. You feel history in your bones. Your strength is wisdom against recklessness. Your risk: romanticising the past.

archetypes.name: John Stuart Mill
archetypes.tagline: The greatest good for the greatest number — measured carefully.
archetypes.description: You want collective solutions to inequality but insist on protecting individual liberty alongside them. Mill invented the framework for weighing the greatest good — and spent his life arguing that freedom was a precondition for it. You are systematic and open to evidence. Your strength is building fairer systems. Your risk: utilitarianism can justify too much.

archetypes.name: Frederick Douglass
archetypes.tagline: Power concedes nothing without a demand. It never did.
archetypes.description: You believe injustice is a collective failure, and you feel it in a way that compels action. Frederick Douglass taught himself to read in secret, escaped slavery, and then spent his life dismantling the system that created it — with words, evidence, and unshakeable moral authority. You are at your best when the stakes are highest. Your risk: burning out or burning bridges.

archetypes.name: John Maynard Keynes
archetypes.tagline: In the long run we are all dead — so fix it now, with data.
archetypes.description: You believe governments have both the obligation and the tools to manage economies rationally, and you are impatient with those who treat suffering as a natural correction. Keynes redesigned the entire global financial architecture at Bretton Woods, invented the policy toolkit that ended the Great Depression, and did it all through rigorous economic modelling rather than ideology. You trust expertise, institutions, and long-term planning. Your strength is that you can actually fix things. Your risk: assuming the experts are always right.

archetypes.name: Simón Bolívar
archetypes.tagline: A people who love freedom will in the end be free.
archetypes.description: You believe in collective liberation, feel the injustice of power acutely, and are willing to impose order in service of a higher purpose. Bolívar freed six nations, wrote constitutions in field tents, and became dictator to hold what he'd won — believing, with some reason, that only he could. You are capable of transformational action. Your risk: the vanguard can become what it defeated.

questions.statement: Essential services like water, electricity, and railways should be owned and run by the government.
The free market, left alone, generally produces better outcomes than government intervention.
The government should set maximum prices on essential goods during a crisis.
Reducing taxes and regulations is the best way to create jobs.
Universal basic income funded by taxpayers is a good idea for a modern economy.
Private hospitals and schools generally provide better quality than government ones.
Large corporations should be taxed heavily to fund public welfare.
If a government-run company is inefficient, it should be privatised rather than reformed.
The wealth gap is primarily a failure of government policy, not individual effort.
Entrepreneurs who take risks and build businesses deserve to keep the majority of their profits.
A strong leader who can act quickly is more important than slow democratic processes.
The government should be able to monitor private communications if it helps prevent crime.
People should be free to say things that most of society finds offensive.
National security is more important than individual privacy.
Citizens should always have the right to protest, even if it disrupts daily life.
Some information is too dangerous for the general public and should be restricted.
No government should have the power to shut down the internet, even temporarily.
It is reasonable for the government to require citizens to carry identification at all times.
People should be able to choose their own lifestyle without societal or legal judgement.
Courts should hand down harsher sentences to deter crime, even for first-time offenders.
When I read a news story, my emotional reaction usually tells me whether it is true.
I would change my opinion on an issue if I saw strong enough data contradicting it.
An article with a personal, emotional story is more convincing than one filled with statistics.
I regularly read news from sources I disagree with.
If most people around me believe something, it is probably true.
Before forming an opinion on a new topic, I try to find at least three independent sources.
I am more likely to share a news article if the headline makes me feel angry or excited.
I can usually tell the difference between an opinion column and a factual news report.
A confident, decisive speaker is usually more trustworthy than someone who qualifies every statement.
I have changed my mind on an important issue in the last two years because of new evidence.
